figures.gloss: The Vedic Sky Father — by name the exact cognate of Zeus-patēr and Jupiter, though already fading in the Rig Veda.
Wielder of the vajra (thunderbolt), king of the gods, who slays the drought-serpent Vṛtra to release the waters.
The first man to die — who therefore became lord of the dead and judge of the road they walk.
The divine artisan who shapes all forms in the womb and forges Indra's thunderbolt.
The sun-god, eye of the heavens, drawn across the day by seven horses.
The Great Goddess in all her forms — gentle Parvati, fierce Durga, devouring Kali — the śakti that powers the gods.
figures.facet: One goddess who is every goddess: divinity that refuses to stay a single face.

figures.gloss: Consciousness and nature — the still witness and the dancing world — whose interplay is the whole of manifest existence.
The golden world-mountain at the center of the universe, around which sun, moon and stars revolve.
The one reality behind all — and the self within (Ātman) is identical to it. Tat tvam asi: thou art that.
figures.facet: States outright what the mirror only assembles: the many are one, and you are it.

figures.gloss: The Vedic Ásura sovereign — guardian of ṛta, the cosmic order; lord of the encompassing night-sky and the cosmic waters, who binds the false with his noose and sees every deceit.
figures.facet: Sovereignty as moral surveillance, not storm: the god you cannot lie to. As the great Ásura he is the seam to Iran's Ahura — one word ennobled on one side of the mountains and demonised on the other.

figures.gloss: God of the covenant, the contract and the sworn bond — the daylight friend who holds agreements inviolable; with Varuna he forms the dual sovereignty Mitra-Váruṇa, day beside night.
figures.facet: The handshake made divine. His name travels west as Iran's Mithra and Rome's Mithras — the contract-god who hardens into a sun.

figures.gloss: The sacrificial fire made god — mouth of the gods and messenger between earth and heaven, who carries the offering up in smoke and the dead through the pyre; kindled new on every hearth and altar.
figures.facet: The go-between the whole rite depends on: no fire, no message reaches the gods. His Iranian brother is Ātar, the same tended flame.

figures.gloss: The auspicious destroyer — ascetic on the mountain, lord of the dance whose tāṇḍava ends and remakes the age; as Paśupati, 'Lord of Animals', master of the beasts and the wild breath beneath civilisation.
figures.facet: Destruction as the hinge of renewal, and the wild owned rather than slain. With Śakti he is the two-in-one (Ardhanārīśvara); as Paśupati his seal looks back to the Indus, older than the Veda that names him.

edges.note: Dyáuṣ-pitṛ́ — the Vedic form that first proved the whole family is one.
Both inherit the reconstructed PIE storm-god's serpent-slaying myth (*Perkʷūnos vs. the serpent).
Storm-hero slays the many-headed/coiling serpent to free the waters / win the realm.
The world-axis as cosmic tree / cosmic mountain — the still center binding the realms.
Center-tree and center-mountain: the same quincunx of four quarters around a holy middle.
A single sacred reality of which all gods and things are passing faces.
The one source behind the ten-thousand things, knowable only by ceasing to stand outside it.
Mitra-Váruṇa: the bound dual sovereignty of Vedic religion — the near and the far, contract and cosmic law, invoked almost always as a single dvandva breath.
Śiva and Śakti — the still ascetic and the dancing power, joined as Ardhanārīśvara, one body half-male and half-female: complementarity made flesh.
Pótnia Thērôn and Paśupati — the Mistress and the Lord of Animals, the same archaic office of the one who owns the wild and its beasts, split into a goddess and a god.

An exact inversion: Indra SLAYS the serpent to release the waters, while the Rainbow Serpent IS the waters and must be honoured, not killed. The same serpent-and-water knot read from opposite ends — dragon-slaying versus serpent-keeping.

A faint touch: both point to an underlying timeless reality that ordinary time and self are surface-ripples upon — the everywhen and Brahman — but the Dreaming is land-bound, ancestral, and plural where Brahman is one absolute consciousness. Flagged as a stretch.

A pointed inversion of gender: Surya is the masculine solar charioteer of the Indo-European world, Yhi the sun-WOMAN whose waking begins life — the same daily sun divinised at opposite poles of sex, the divergence itself the lesson.

Both are the sacred hearth/altar fire that carries human offerings to the gods — the fire as messenger and mouth of sacrifice. Kamuy-huci diverges as an aged grandmother rather than a youthful priest-god, and stands at a wholly separate, non-Indo-European root; convergent function, no descent.

A loose reach to Shiva as Pashupati, 'lord of beasts' — the master-of-animals role meets across vast distance, but the Ainu mountain-bear is a hunted guest, not a great ascetic god; flagged as speculative.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: Both are the ultimate, attribute-less reality underlying the diverse emanations, avatars, and cycles of the physical universe.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: The Tagalog supreme god absorbed the Sanskrit title 'Bhattara' (noble lord) via Srivijayan and Majapahit trade networks.
The axis-mundi shared: a still central point the faithful orient toward and circumambulate, as cosmologies the world over fix their centre on a holy mountain or stone.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: The invocation of divine fire as the supreme active agent that consumes the dross of human karma and mediates between the physical and etheric realms.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: The absolute, undifferentiated divine reality that serves as the ultimate source of the individual self, completely devoid of anthropomorphic judgment.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: Akilam equates Ekam with the ultimate unmanifest Brahman, though reimagined as accessible without Brahminical mediation.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: Akilam claims Vaikundar subordinates and absorbs the powers of the traditional Shaivite pantheon to establish a new universal order.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: Warrior deities who slay a primordial serpent or sibling right after birth to establish cosmic dominance and order.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: The singular, panentheistic cosmic energy from which all individual gods and physical reality are mere transient manifestations.
GOLD SEAM: Deeply parallels Nirguna Brahman—the unmanifest, attribute-less absolute reality behind all cosmic illusion.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: Acintya is historically and theologically identified as Paramashiva, the supreme, unmanifest form of Shiva in the Shaiva Siddhanta tradition.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: Modern Balinese Hindu theology explicitly equates Acintya with the Upanishadic concept of Brahman to unify its pantheon.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: Rangda is formally syncretized in Balinese theology with Durga and Kali, the fierce, terrifying aspects of Parvati.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: The Padmasana is explicitly designed to be the localized, physical manifestation of the cosmic Mount Meru.
Dievas and Vedic Dyaus continue the same *Dyeus — the cognate star at its widest span.
The thunder-god who slays the serpent: Perkūnas hunting Velnias rhymes structurally with Indra and Vṛtra, though the names are not cognate.
Both drive the solar chariot across the sky and both belong to IE solar imagery, but Saulė is mother where Sūrya is lord — the gender inverts.
First-and-foremost lords of the dead in IE daughter traditions; the Latvian Veļi (the dead) gather under Velnias as the departed gather under Yama.

The nine-storey central world-mountain with four quarter-rivers parallels Meru at the centre of its cosmos; shared Inner-Asian/Indic axis-mountain idiom and likely some Buddhist-era cross-modelling, but stamped uniquely with the Bön swastika rather than inherited by descent.

The guided journey of the soul toward its assignment after death rhymes with the Vedic-derived lord-of-the-dead; here the office is met as a passage and a ransom rather than a throne, and only in the later stratum.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: The explicit re-appropriation of the classical Advaita absolute, but recast as an active, personal, yet formless moral creator.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: The literal continuation of the Vedic Agni ritual, radically repurposed to bypass Brahmin priests.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: Dayananda explicitly anchors his theology in the Vedic concepts of Purusha (God/Soul) and Prakriti (Matter), insisting on their eternal separation.

Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: Despite Buddhist rejections of a permanent Atman, Tathagatagarbha structurally functions much like Brahman—an eternal, inherent purity underlying the illusion of phenomenal reality.

Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: The Tantric 'Yab-Yum' depiction of Yidams in sexual union perfectly mirrors the union of Purusha (consciousness/method) and Prakriti (matter/wisdom) to generate enlightenment.
Sumeru IS Meru — the same world-mountain, the same Sanskrit name (su-meru, 'excellent Meru'), carried from Vedic into Buddhist cosmology as direct Indic inheritance. The brightest seam in the shard.
One and the same deity by descent: Vedic Yama, first-mortal and king of the dead, inherited wholesale into Buddhism and carried east as Yanluo / Enma. Indic-internal cognate.
Śakra is Indra — the Vedic storm-king himself, name and vajra retained, but reduced to an impermanent heaven-god subject to rebirth. Direct inheritance, then demotion.

The load-bearing divergence: anātman is the explicit DENIAL of the Brahman/Ātman of the Upaniṣads — same Indic conversation, opposite verdict. No eternal self, no world-soul. Tiered 1 because the contradiction is a documented historical argument within one tradition, not a loose parallel.
Against the Sāṃkhya eternal Puruṣa (the witness-self), Buddhism denies any abiding witness — a documented doctrinal dispute between Indian schools.

Both name the one reality appearing as the many — yet they diverge at the root: Brahman is the eternal Self the trikāya's dharmakāya pointedly is NOT. Kinship in structure, documented opposition in content.

The same god, inverted in rank: Vedic Indra is the supreme storm-king; Buddhist Śakra is the same figure demoted to an impermanent, unenlightened disciple — a documented theological re-ranking within Indic religion.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: Both philosophies assert that consciousness is the fundamental bedrock of existence, and that matter is merely its localized projection.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: The ultimate, undifferentiated reality that stands behind the multiplicity of the created universe.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: The conceptual union of pure consciousness and raw, active material nature churning the universe into existence.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: A mythic central mountain functioning as the absolute axis mundi and the bridge between terrestrial and celestial realms.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: The sacred fire personified as a divine guest and active ancestral presence within the domestic hearth.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: Parallel assertions of ultimate non-dualism; the realization that the individual self is inherently indistinguishable from the cosmic all.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: The pairing of CAW's Gaia and Horned God loosely mirrors the ancient division of cosmic reality into a vast foundational matrix and an activating biological principle.
GOLD SEAM: The ultimate, pervasive, and unmanifest reality that serves as the ontological ground of all moral and cosmic order.
GOLD SEAM: The cosmic sovereign force that surveys human morality and enforces ethical law from the heavens, punishing the wicked and uplifting the just.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: The double-sexed primordial entity whose violent dismemberment provides the raw material for the world's generation.
The world-axis as the meeting of the realms — the Batang Garing and the cosmic mountain both centre the layered cosmos.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: Historically syncretized by ancient Greek ambassadors like Megasthenes, who explicitly identified the ecstatic, mountain-dwelling Lord of Dance with Dionysus.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: The primordial divine being whose violent dismemberment provides the raw material and spiritual substrate for humanity's genesis.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: The structural mirroring of the universe; just as the cosmos is the body of Purusha, the astrological map treats the sky as a projected human macrocosm.
A primordial being sacrificed and parcelled out so that the ordered cosmos and its categories arise from his body — primordial-sacrifice as the engine of creation.
The single ground from which all categories of being unfold by inward vibration rather than external act; a reach toward Brahman as the source, flagged.
A stepped, tiered cosmic structure that maps every direction and being onto an axis; a reach across families, flagged.

Murugan (Cēyōṉ, 'the Red One') is a solar hill-god identified with the Sanskrit Skanda-Kārttikeya — a documented Sanskritisation in which the indigenous Tamil deity was overlaid with the war-god of the Veda, the seam where the Dravidian shard meets the Vedic one.

The young warrior-god who splits the demon-mountain with his weapon rhymes with Indra cleaving Vala / the mountains to free the waters — the dragon-slayer / mountain-breaker pattern, reached on the Tamil side through Murugan's lancing of Krauñca.

Koṟṟavai, the Tamil mother of war and victory, was historically assimilated into the Sanskrit Durgā / Kālī (a face of Pārvatī/Devī) — a documented layering by which the indigenous goddess was reidentified as the pan-Indian demon-slayer.

The fierce Amman goddesses were partially drawn into the Sanskrit Devī as local forms of the great goddess — a documented Brahmanical assimilation of village mothers, though Māriyammaṉ's disease-and-protection theology stays largely outside the canon.

The doctrine that all the village mothers are forms of one Goddess maps onto the Devī theology that makes every local goddess a face of the single Mahādevī — the Sanskrit frame that absorbed the Tamil multiplicity, a documented assimilation.

Both place an impersonal sacred behind the personal gods — yet they diverge sharply: Brahman is serene, deathless, one with the self, while aṉaṅku is perilous, ambient and to be contained. Kinship of structure (a power beneath the deities), opposite in temperament.

Poṅkal is timed to the sun's northward turn (the Makara / Uttarāyaṇa transition) honoured across Indic religion — the Tamil harvest-overflow rite sharing the solar calendar that Sūrya governs, a documented pan-Indic solar reckoning.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: The Sugmad strongly parallels the Nirguna Brahman of Advaita Vedanta—an absolute, unqualifiable reality beyond all attributes.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: Both serve as the lords of death, karma, and time, administering justice in the lower realms without being inherently 'evil'.
GOLD SEAM: Absolute lords of death and uncompromising arbiters of final justice.
GOLD SEAM: The solar orb conceived not just as light, but as the physical manifestation of cosmic justice and order.
GOLD SEAM: Architectural and cosmological mountains serving as the supreme axis connecting heaven and earth.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: Both are consumed physical elements that act as the active, living mediator between the human and divine realms.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: The UDV theology of the vine and leaf parallels the Vedic dualism of primal force/nature and pure consciousness/light.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: Both signify a supreme reality of eternal bliss that is entirely devoid of personal, intervening characteristics or desires.
Both join wisdom/craft to the thunder-weapon's making — Menrva wields the bolt, Tvastr forges Indra's; the smith near the storm.
Both are cosmic artificers who shape the very vessels and frame of the world; an honest structural parallel across Uralic and Indo-European with no descent between them.
The cosmic egg whose halves become heaven and earth echoes the Hiraṇyagarbha / world-egg of Indic thought — one of humanity's most widely scattered cosmogonic images, here with no shared line.
The creating pair as the male and female poles whose union produces the lived world — structural rhyme with Purusa and Prakrti.
Divine artificer who shapes the implements of the world — the smith-joint across families.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: The ultimate, undifferentiated reality that stands behind the mechanical illusion and multiplicity of the created universe.
GOLD SEAM: The ultimate, impersonal ground of reality that unifies existence and transcends all sectarian dogma.

Both are the formless Absolute behind all manifestation, and the gnostic spark that IS the One rhymes with Atman that is Brahman — knowing the self as the All. No descent claimed, a deep structural cousinhood.

The Samkhya purusa, pure consciousness mistakenly entangled in prakrti and freed by discriminating knowledge, mirrors the spark trapped in matter and freed by gnosis — salvation as KNOWING, in two distant traditions.
Microcosm-equals-macrocosm rhymes with the Upanishadic identity of Atman and Brahman — the small self found to BE the cosmic whole, correspondence raised to identity.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: Both enforce the inviolable cosmic law that binds entities through oaths and true speech.
GOLD SEAM: The iconic Harappan ascetic is widely argued by archaeologists to be the direct historical and structural precursor to the Vedic and Hindu Shiva Pashupati.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: Both are the impersonal, ineffable ultimate reality hiding behind a pantheon of colorful, functional deities.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: Both embody the heavy, restricting, and inescapable finality of time and the karmic limits placed on mortals.

Both point past the gods to a single underlying power, but loosely — Brahman is the absolute ground of being, orenda the contending potency one thing wields against another; the one-through-many family, but orenda is plural and agonistic where Brahman is one and still.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: A classic Dumézilian parallel where both are sovereign, magically-binding sky gods whose authority rests on occult knowledge and fetters rather than martial combat.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: An impersonal, absolute, and limitless divine reality that transcends all attributes and forms.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: The primordial, macrocosmic anthropomorphic blueprint from whose metaphysical body the universe is constructed.
GOLD SEAM: The Nous is the universal intellect that is both transcendent and immanent — analogous to Brahman, the ultimate reality that is the ground of all being. Both are the goal of gnosis.

GOLD SEAM: The Anthropos is the primordial human who is the image of the Nous — analogous to Purusha, the cosmic man whose body becomes the universe in the Rig Veda. Both are androgynous and both are the source of creation.
Shared Indo-European storm-and-serpent inheritance: as Indra slays Vrtra to release the waters, Tarhunna slays Illuyanka to restore the land — the same Chaoskampf from a common reconstructed root.
GOLD SEAM: The boisterous, weapon-wielding storm king who slays the cosmic serpent to establish celestial order.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: Direct linguistic cognate linking the Illyrian and Vedic ancient sky-fathers.

A speculative reach: Sila as encompassing world-awareness in which the self participates, faintly echoing Brahman as the pervading ground — but Sila is weather and breath, never a metaphysical absolute.
GOLD SEAM: The ultimate, non-dual reality underpinning all existence, a parallel drawn explicitly in later Persian and Indian Sufi metaphysics (Wahdat al-Wujud).
Both Indic cosmologies set the lived world inside vast recurring world-ages; the Vedic yuga-cycle and the Jain kāla-cakra are parallel readings of declining and renewing time.

Against the Upaniṣadic merger of Ātman into one Brahman, Jainism insists souls stay eternally MANY and distinct even when freed — a deliberate doctrinal counter to Vedic non-dualism it grew up beside.

Both split reality into conscious spirit and unconscious matter — jīva/ajīva mirrors puruṣa/prakṛti as Indic spirit-matter dualisms, though Jainism multiplies the souls where Sāṃkhya keeps puruṣas plural too.

Jainism keeps the Indic three-world axis and the world-mountain but evicts the gods from its peak — where Meru is crowned by Brahmā's heaven, the Loka's summit holds only liberated souls and no creator anywhere.
The counter-shard at its sharpest: where Vedānta makes the cosmos the play of one absolute, Jainism denies any ground-of-being — only eternal substances and law, no One behind the many.

The cosmic-man shape echoes the Vedic Puruṣa whose body becomes the world — but the Jain Loka is no one's body and is never sacrificed; the form is borrowed, the primordial-sacrifice pointedly absent.
Where Advaita resolves all standpoints into one undivided truth, anekāntavāda holds that no standpoint is ever final — pluralism of perspective set against non-dual unity.

Liberation as the great divider: the Jain soul rises to the world's ceiling and abides forever ITSELF, while the Vedic ātman is freed by dissolving INTO Brahman — release as persistence versus release as merger.

Both are sky-fathers and creators — but Dyaus Pita is a fading figure in the Vedic pantheon, while YHWH is the sole sovereign of a monotheistic faith. A loose structural echo of the sky-father joint, flagged.
GOLD SEAM: The parallel to Nirguna Brahman—the ultimate, non-dual, attribute-less reality underpinning all existence.
GOLD SEAM: The vision of a primordial, macrocosmic 'Person' whose spiritual anatomy serves as the structural blueprint for the entire universe.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: The name 'Mahandeo' is a direct linguistic cognate of Sanskrit 'Maha-deva' (Shiva), preserving an archaic, pre-ascetic warrior-chief function.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: A heroic, horse-riding figure arriving from afar to break stagnation, distribute vitality, and defeat the forces of winter.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: The divine presence anchored directly within the domestic hearth fire, acting as the spiritual center of the family.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: The supreme deity was heavily identified with Shiva (Batara Guru) during the Hindu-Buddhist period of Java.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: Syncretized with the ultimate Hindu cosmic principle through Majapahit court theology.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: The Javanese shadow puppet physically represents and descends directly from Hindu-Buddhist cosmology of Mount Meru.

Loose: both personify the sun, but Surya is a radiant charioteer-god of high honour while Sores is a fierce heat ranked BELOW the dawn and moon — the solar joint shared, the valence inverted. Flagged to mark the divergence: the Khoekhoe sun is a hazard, not a king.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: A towering peak conceptualized not just as a mountain, but as the literal axis and anchor of the cosmos.
The original androgyne / dual principle whose splitting begins the lived world — a loose echo across unrelated families, flagged.

The sacred mountain as the meeting of worlds — Korean sansin worship survives inside Buddhism much as the cosmic-mountain idea was carried in by it; parallel of the holy height, partly entangled by historical Buddhist contact.
A loose first-ancestor parallel: both founding beings from whom a people springs, but Dangun is a born hybrid king, not a sacrificed cosmic body — flagged, the seam tiered down.
The sacred totality of which all beings are aspects — Wakáŋ Tȟáŋka's sixteen-in-one answers Brahman's one-without-a-second behind the many; both resist the idol of a single ruling Person.
The sun as the wheeling holy eye and patron of high virtue, gazed upon in disciplined devotion; Wí and Sūrya are independent solar foci of a sky-revering people.

Storm-wielders whose thunderbolt war with a water-withholding serpent-monster keeps the world in order — Wakíŋyaŋ against Uŋktéȟi, Indra against Vṛtra; the same sky-against-deep drama, no shared line.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: Lingayat theology explicitly identifies the Upanishadic Brahman with Parasiva, the supreme, non-dual absolute.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: The direct theological identification with the Vedic/Puranic Shiva, though completely stripped of his traditional mythology and temple-bound iconography.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: Both represent the deification of the binding oath, the contract, and the societal glue that holds the warrior community together.
GOLD SEAM: Both traditions feature a primordial being whose fragmentation seeds the universe, though in Manichaeism this is a wartime casualty, not a harmonious sacrifice.

Both discharge the order-restoring, water-freeing hero-function against a serpent of the deep — but Treng-Treng does it as a fellow snake hoisting the mountain above the flood, where Indra splits Vṛtra with the bolt; the chaoskampf template reached and inverted in the means.

Both stack a quartered, directional sacred cosmos above the lived world; a loose parallel of the four-cornered layered heaven, the Wenu Mapu as surveyed 'land above' against Meru's cosmic mountain, no contact implied.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: Martial weather deities essential for releasing the waters needed for agricultural survival.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: A primordial cosmic being whose dismemberment generates the literal components of the physical universe.
GOLD SEAM: Both are solar gods who drive a chariot across the sky — Sol Invictus is the Roman sun, Surya is the Vedic sun. Both are associated with light, truth, and the daily cycle.

GOLD SEAM: Both are primordial beings whose sacrifice creates the cosmos — the bull's blood becomes grain and wine, Purusha's body becomes the four varnas and the cosmos. The structure of sacrifice-as-creation is identical.

Both are figures of time and destruction — the Leontocephalus is the devouring maw of eternity, Shiva is the destroyer who dances the cosmos into and out of existence. A loose parallel of the terrifying aspect of time, flagged.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: Modern ADF Druidry explicitly modeled its Sacred Fire rituals on Vedic Agni, utilizing comparative Indo-European scholarship to reconstruct a functional hearth cult.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: Specifically mirroring Shiva in his aspect as Pashupati (Lord of Animals), both are horned or animal-associated lords representing untamed male power in the wilderness.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: ISKCON explicitly subordinates the traditional formless Brahman, redefining it merely as the shining bodily effulgence radiating from Krishna's supreme personal form.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: Both act as the ultimate, unmanifest cosmic reality standing behind the active, colorful pantheons of lesser gods.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: In later syncretic practice, the physical ovoo was explicitly mapped onto Buddhist Mount Meru cosmology.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: Both are the deified hearth fire acting as the central axis of household ritual and the receiver of offerings.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: Both personify the sacred ritual fire that consumes first-fruit offerings and acts as the crucial intermediary and witness between humans and the divine.
GOLD SEAM: The theological impulse to perfectly balance a supreme male principle with a direct, nominal feminine counterpart.
GOLD SEAM: Sovereign lords of the deep, earthly waters and foundational cosmic oaths who stand in tension with the active, lightning-wielding sky deities.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: Shares the Vedic Indra's thunderbolt weapon and status as a storm deity, though thoroughly pacified by Buddhist cosmology.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: The 'Arm Leg Leg Arm Head' doctrine conceptualizes the Supreme Being structurally as a Cosmic Man, much like the Vedic Purusha whose body comprises the universe.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: Both represent the transformative, living fire serving as the essential and active intermediary between humanity and the divine.

Lightning-armed slayers of the monster that blocks the world's life — Monster Slayer with his bolt-arrows beheading Yéʼiitsoh as Indra wields the vajra against Vṛtra; the dragon-slayer template arrived at far outside Indo-European.
Solar deities who father heroic sons and serve as the sky's measure; the Sun-Bearer's demanding paternity of the twins rhymes with Sūrya siring his sons, a parallel solar fatherhood.
Sacred mountains as the structuring centre of the cosmos; the four Diné peaks bound the proper world as Meru axes the Vedic one — the world-mountain motif, four-fold here, central there.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: The ultimate, non-dual source of reality from which all phenomenal existence or illusion springs, fundamentally beyond predication.
GOLD SEAM: Matches the Upanishadic equation of Atman and Brahman: the innermost self is completely identical to the ultimate reality.
GOLD SEAM: Both posit an ultimate, infinite, non-personal reality of which the material world and individual souls are merely expressions or reflections.
GOLD SEAM: The solitary, sacred mountain recognized not just as a high place, but as the literal, physical axis of the universe.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: Both are the ultimate, formless cosmic principles that undergird the multiplicity of the visible world and its lesser, active deities.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: Both represent the first mortal to die, whose tragic death establishes the path to the afterlife and who subsequently becomes its benevolent, sovereign guide.
Storm-power locked in combat with a great water-beast (Thunderbird/whale ~ Indra/Vrtra) — the resemblance is real but distant; flagged.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: Both posit a single, infinite, all-encompassing reality that constitutes the entirety of existence.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: Primordial beings whose bodily sacrifice directly constructs the physical universe, flora, fauna, and human social categories.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: An impersonal, all-encompassing metaphysical reality that binds the universe, which the enlightened mind seeks to merge with harmoniously.
GOLD SEAM: Sovereign lords of the deep and the heavens who act as the ultimate, inescapable witnesses to treaties and oaths.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: The ontological pairing of an active, limiting, ordering principle against a boundless, passive material substrate.
GOLD SEAM: The absolute spiritual and physical center of the universe, serving as the orienting homeland for the righteous.
GOLD SEAM: The supreme, unifying reality that dissolves the illusion of separation between the self and the cosmos.
GOLD SEAM: The essential divine feminine consort who anchors and completes the supreme male deity in a cosmic partnership.
GOLD SEAM: Both act as the supreme geometric and spiritual center of the universe, anchoring the macrocosmic order.

The thunder-wielder who shatters the rock and looses the waters/weather is one of humanity's most widespread divine offices; Tiermes meets Indra at the storm-wielder joint by independent road, no descent between Uralic and Indo-Aryan.
Inverted and loose: Indra slays a serpent to RELEASE the waters, while the San shaman slays the water-animal itself to MAKE the rain — the rain-by-slaughter motif read from opposite ends. Flagged.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: The necessary cosmic union of a male spiritual principle (the sun) and a female material principle (the earth) to generate life.

GOLD SEAM: While Vedanta sees ultimate liberation as the dissolution of the individual self into a single universal consciousness, Scientology insists on the eternal individuality of liberated thetans.
GOLD SEAM: MEST precisely parallels Prakriti as the dense, entangling matrix of matter that traps the pure consciousness of the thetan (Purusha).
GOLD SEAM: The primordial, animating sacred fire of the Indo-Iranian sphere that binds oaths and purifies the community.
GOLD SEAM: Sovereign lords of the waters heavily associated with elite, aristocratic oversight rather than common tribal worship.
GOLD SEAM: The ancient Indo-Iranian mythic complex of the first mortal and genealogical progenitor.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: The ultimate, all-encompassing substance of reality from which all temporary forms arise and eventually return.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: The explicit historical elevation of the Vedic storm-and-wilderness deity into the absolute metaphysical substrate of the universe.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: The philosophical abstraction of divine energy fully merged with the mythological figure of the mountain goddess.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: The dual principles of consciousness and materiality/energy, though Shaivism fundamentally unites them into a single non-dual continuum.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: Shaktism deliberately absorbs the Samkhya dualism, elevating Prakriti (matter/energy) to be both the active force AND the ultimate consciousness.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: The historical and theological identification of the active Goddess with the formless, absolute Brahman of the Upanishads.

GOLD SEAM: The Sikh Gurus actively utilized the Vedantic vocabulary of a supreme, unmanifest reality ('Nirguna Brahman'), while radically reorienting it toward active, loving devotion rather than ascetic withdrawal.
GOLD SEAM: The philosophical pairing of the eternal, observing spirit with the active, material world, demanding mastery of both rather than the abandonment of the temporal.
GOLD SEAM: Both represent an impersonal, foundational cosmic reality that underpins the universe, distinct from a judging creator.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: The ultimate, non-dual source of reality that permeates the phenomenal world, identical with the universe itself yet acting as its supreme consciousness.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: The cosmic man concept—the Vedic Purusha and the Swedenborgian Grand Man both envision the totality of existence as a single immense divine body.
Both the warm near mother-power complementing a remote supreme; analogous function, no descent or contact claimed.
Through Tibetan-Mongolian Buddhism, Erlik is directly identified with Yama (Erlik Nomun Khan) as judge of the dead — a documented religious syncretism, not a mere parallel.
Both the vertical axis about which the heavens revolve, mirrored in built ritual form (sky-pillar / cosmic mountain); some of the layered-cosmos detail entered Altai cosmology via Buddhism.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: The central pillar and precise geospatial center of the physical and spiritual universe around which reality revolves.
GOLD SEAM: Nuit mirrors Prakriti as the vast, passive matrix of all possibility that is only animated by the active point of consciousness (Hadit/Purusha).
GOLD SEAM: Blavatsky explicitly adopted the Vedantic concept of Brahman as the foundation for her impersonal Absolute.
GOLD SEAM: Blavatsky specifically identified Fohat with Agni, viewing the Vedic fire god as an allegory for cosmic electricity.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: Maharishi explicitly identified pure consciousness with the Vedantic absolute, Brahman.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: Both traditions declare that the physical universe is ultimately an illusion (Maya/Simulation) generated by a deeper, underlying code or absolute reality.
GOLD SEAM: Both serve as the ultimate cosmic axis where the higher realm of the gods physically and structurally intersects with the terrestrial plane.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: The monistic reality underlying all forms; the universal spirit into which the individual soul is entirely integrated without a mediator.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: A loose aesthetic parallel to the union of pluralities, generating the communal body through wildly diverse, individuated contributions.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: The literal, stationary geographic axis around which all physical and spiritual realms revolve.
GOLD SEAM: The radiant solar deity representing cosmic order, truth, and the all-seeing eye of the heavens.
GOLD SEAM: The sacred mountain as the literal, physical dwelling place and structural center of the divine realm.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: The historical evolution of the Vedic sun god's world-encompassing strides into the all-pervading Supreme Being.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: The deliberate theological absorption of the Vedic storm king's role as the supreme demon-conqueror and protector of the worlds.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: The philosophical mapping of Lakshmi onto Prakriti, identifying her as the active, material energy through which the passive supreme consciousness acts.
GOLD SEAM: The Wiccan Goddess functions much like Prakriti—the dynamic, active, material matrix of the universe—against the God's secondary polarity.
GOLD SEAM: Both are horned, untamed lords of beasts and marginal spaces who embody raw generative power intertwined with necessary destruction.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: The geographical and metaphysical anchor of the universe, serving as the throne of the highest spiritual authority.
GOLD SEAM: The sun as a pure, divine eye and the crucial focal point for daily orienting prayers.
Both solar guardians, but the true cognate is to Mitra not Surya; here a functional solar parallel only.

Avestan Yima and Vedic Yama descend from a common Indo-Iranian *Yama, 'twin' — but Iran keeps him as FIRST KING and ark-builder while India makes him first to DIE and lord of the dead: a cognate that inverts function.
The cognate's divergence: Iran's Yima preserves life through the winter; India's Yama is the first mortal and king of the underworld. Same name, opposite valence of death.

The great Ahura/Asura inversion: Avestan 'ahura' and Vedic 'asura' are cognate, but Iran exalts the ahuras as good and demonises the daevas, while India exalts the devas and demonises the asuras — cognate words, flipped valence. (No standalone asura figure id listed; seam noted home.)
Both inherit the Indo-Iranian high-sky lordship, but Ahura Mazda is moralised into an ethical creator-absolute rather than a passive Father Sky.
Long-argued kinship of the Wise Lord with the Vedic asura Varuna, sovereign guardian of rta and the oath — structurally the closest Vedic match. (Varuna not in listed ids; noted as scholarly link.)

Tier-1 Indo-Iranian cognate: Avestan Mithra and Vedic Mitra, both gods of the covenant/contract, from a shared Indo-Iranian deity of the binding bond. (Mitra not in listed ids; the seam's home is the Vedic shard.)
Indo-Iranian fire-cult kinship: both tend a divine sacrificial fire as messenger between worlds, though Iran refuses to treat the flame as a devouring idol. (Agni not in listed ids.)
Asa as cosmic Truth shares the cosmic-order joint with the Vedic absolute; the firmer cognate, though, is Asa to rta, not to Brahman.

A reach: Unkulunkulu as first cosmic man from whom humankind breaks off faintly echoes the Purusa whose body seeds the world — but there is no sacrifice here, only a snapping-off. Flagged as speculative.
The sky's daughter as great-mother of fertility and abundance; both gather the powers of growth and the rains into a single nurturing goddess.

A loose link: Yama is the first man to die who becomes lord of the dead, while the amadlozi are the collective dead who govern the living — both make the deceased a continuing power, by different routes.
Storm and serpent meet in both, but inverted: Indra SLAYS the serpent Vrtra to release the rains, while Inkanyamba IS the serpent that brings them — the dragon-fight read backwards.

dossier.essence: The religion of the Vedas, the sacrificial cult of the Indo-Aryan peoples of the northwestern Indian subcontinent from roughly the mid-second millennium BCE, together with the vast Brahmanical and Hindu tradition that grew continuously out of it. Early Vedic religion centered on hymns and fire-sacrifice to a pantheon of mostly male sky and nature gods (Indra, Agni, Varuna, Surya); over the following millennia it transformed into the temple-, devotion-, and philosophy-based traditions now called Hinduism, without a single founder or creed.
dossier.cosmos: Vedic thought is governed by rta, the cosmic order upheld by the gods and by correct sacrifice. The Upanishads (from c. 800 BCE) reframe the goal as knowledge of Brahman, the one reality, identical with the inmost self (atman): tat tvam asi, 'thou art that.' The soul is bound to rebirth (samsara) by karma until it attains liberation (moksha), and the universe turns through immense cycles of creation and dissolution oriented around the world-mountain Meru.
dossier.practice: The earliest layer centers on shrauta fire-sacrifice performed by Brahmin priests reciting Vedic hymns, alongside domestic (grihya) rites and life-cycle sacraments (samskaras) that persist to the present. Later Hindu practice adds temple image-worship (puja), pilgrimage, festival, devotion (bhakti), and the disciplines of yoga and renunciation. Ritual authority traditionally rests with the Brahmin priesthood and the framework of varna (class) and ashrama (life-stage) duty (dharma).
dossier.canon: The four Vedas (Rig, Yajur, Sama, Atharva) with their Brahmanas, Aranyakas, and Upanishads, regarded as shruti ('that which is heard,' revealed) and transmitted with extraordinary oral precision; later supplemented by smriti literature, the epics (Mahabharata, including the Bhagavad Gita; Ramayana), the Puranas, and the Dharmashastras.
dossier.reflects: It foregrounds the sacrificial and then the metaphysical impulse pushed to its furthest, from a cosmos sustained by exact ritual to the claim that the many are ultimately one and the self is that one.
sources: Stephanie W. Jamison & Joel P. Brereton, 'The Rigveda: The Earliest Religious Poetry of India' (2014)
Jan Gonda, 'Vedic Literature: Samhitas and Brahmanas' (1975)
